text: In 2016, researchers at the Oxford Internet Institute documented the systematic use of "computational propaganda" — automated social media accounts deployed to amplify political messages during elections across seventeen countries. What distinguished this from older propaganda was scale and personalization: algorithms could identify micro-segments of the electorate susceptible to specific emotional appeals and deliver targeted messages at volumes no human operation could match. Political scientist Philip Howard described the result as a new kind of power asymmetry: one in which well-resourced actors can steer the attention of millions while those millions have no equivalent mechanism for collective response, creating a structural imbalance in democratic deliberation.
question: According to Philip Howard, what power asymmetry does computational propaganda create?

options.text: Governments can now monitor citizen communications faster than citizens can organize
Wealthy actors can steer mass attention while citizens lack equivalent collective response capacity
Algorithms amplify extreme viewpoints more effectively than moderate ones
Foreign states can intervene in elections more effectively than domestic political parties
